tasks.title: Delete stale merged branches
tasks.description: Remove feature/game-mode-setting and feature/sqlite-concurrency-overhaul locally and on origin. Both are already merged into main.
tasks.details:
Starting on main with a clean working tree, run:

git branch --show-current   (must output 'main')
git status --short          (must be empty)
git branch --merged main    (confirm both feature/game-mode-setting and feature/sqlite-concurrency-overhaul appear in the merged list; if either is missing STOP and report)

git branch -d feature/game-mode-setting
git branch -d feature/sqlite-concurrency-overhaul
git push origin --delete feature/game-mode-setting
git push origin --delete feature/sqlite-concurrency-overhaul
git remote prune origin

Do NOT use -D (force delete). If -d refuses for either branch, STOP and report - that means it wasn't actually merged and we need to investigate before deleting.
tasks.testStrategy: git branch -a must show only: main, remotes/origin/HEAD -> origin/main, remotes/origin/main. No other local or remote branches. No commit is made on main itself.

tasks.title: Investigate _invalidate_dashboard_static_caches - keep, fix, or remove
tasks.description: Audit notes flagged this helper as 'dead' but it is still defined at main.py:36 and called from main.py:116, 207, 316. Determine whether it is a no-op, functional, or genuinely dead, then act accordingly.
tasks.details:
Read main.py:30-50 for the definition and main.py:100-130, 200-220, 310-325 for the three call sites. For each call site, note what the caller is trying to invalidate and whether the function body actually does anything useful.

Three possible outcomes:

A. The body is a no-op or try/except-pass shield that never runs useful code. Remove the definition and all three call sites. Run tests.

B. The body does real work but nothing in the dashboard currently reads the caches it invalidates (they were removed during the Phase 1 perf sweep). Remove the definition and all three call sites. Run tests.

C. The body does real work and something still depends on it. Leave it alone, add a one-line docstring explaining what it invalidates, and mark this task done with 'kept as-is, documented'.

Deliverable is a short report stating which outcome applied, the diff if any, and the test count before and after. Do NOT rename or refactor beyond removal.
tasks.testStrategy: Run pytest --collect-only -q before and after any change. Test count must stay at 174 or higher. If any code is removed, also start the server once and hit /, /my-fleet, /my-routes, /buy-next, /hub-explorer, /contributions and confirm all 200. No regression in server startup log.

tasks.title: Re-enable hub freshness banners (base_context stub removal)
tasks.description: Replace the 21 base_context(request, None) stub call sites with base_context(request, conn) where conn is the request's read connection, so hub freshness banners render again.
tasks.details:
Grep for base_context(request, None) to locate all 21 sites:
- 20 in dashboard/routes/pages.py (lines 136, 151, 169, 184, 191, 205, 216, 234, 260, 280, 288, 296, 390, 397, 450, 474, 516, 525, 532)
- 1 in dashboard/routes/settings.py (line 58)

For each site, the enclosing handler already has (or can trivially obtain) a read connection via the get_read_db dependency. Thread conn through to base_context. Confirm base_context(request, conn) when given a real conn produces the same HubFreshness context shape the templates originally consumed - grep the templates for the freshness variable names and confirm they are set non-empty in at least one page.

If any handler does NOT currently have a conn in scope, add the dependency to the signature rather than building a new connection inside the handler - the concurrency model requires all reads go through get_read_db.

Scope constraint: this task is ONLY the stub removal plus dependency threading. Do NOT rewrite base_context, do NOT touch the hub freshness SQL, do NOT change templates. If base_context itself is broken under a real conn, STOP and surface it - that is a separate task.
tasks.testStrategy: Start the server and load each affected page once. Confirm: (a) no 500 errors in the log; (b) the hub freshness banner actually appears (or is intentionally absent for valid reasons) on at least /my-hubs, /my-routes, and /; (c) pytest --collect-only reports 174 or higher; (d) pytest -q passes 174/174. Grep after the change: grep -rn 'base_context(request, None)' dashboard/ must return zero hits.

tasks.title: Add engine, mods, purchase_price columns to my_fleet (original task 6a)
tasks.description: Add the three missing my_fleet columns that were specified in the original Add Route task 6a but never shipped. Thread them through the inline fleet entry form, POST /api/routes/add handler, and any read paths that surface fleet details.
tasks.details:
Schema migration in database/schema.py:
- engine TEXT (nullable; string identifier from the am4 engine catalog)
- mods TEXT (nullable; comma-separated mod codes or JSON list - match whatever convention my_routes already uses if any, otherwise comma-separated string)
- purchase_price INTEGER (nullable; in game currency units, matches other price columns)

Migration must be idempotent using the existing _migrate_* pattern (check PRAGMA table_info, ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN if missing). Update SCHEMA_SQL canonical definition too.

Form and handler:
- dashboard/templates/add_route.html inline fleet expansion: add optional engine dropdown (sourced from am4 catalog), mods multi-select or text input, purchase_price number input
- dashboard/routes/api/routes.py POST /api/routes/add: accept the three new fields on inline_fleet payload, persist on my_fleet insert/upsert, all inside the existing transaction

Read paths: grep for places that SELECT from my_fleet and surface row contents - /my-fleet page, Fleet Explorer, any APIs. Add the three new columns to their outputs if they make sense; leave template formatting minimal (just show the raw values).

Values are all optional - existing rows with NULL for the three new columns must continue to work everywhere.
tasks.testStrategy: Run migration on a copy of the live DB and on a fresh DB; PRAGMA table_info(my_fleet) must include the three new columns with correct types. Add an aircraft via the inline fleet form with all three new fields populated, confirm the row persists with correct values (query directly via python). Then add another aircraft leaving the three fields empty and confirm that also works. Load /my-fleet and confirm no template errors. pytest -q must pass, ideally with new tests for the schema migration and the POST handler accepting the new fields (target 176+ tests).

tasks.title: Fix /api/heatmap-panel 7s query (Global Heatmap blank)
tasks.description: The /api/heatmap-panel endpoint runs one query that takes ~7 seconds, almost certainly due to a missing index on the hub/destination join. The slow response is why the Global Heatmap renders blank - the frontend likely times out or the user navigates away.
tasks.details:
Endpoint at dashboard/routes/api/recommendations.py:719-720. Read the handler and extract the single slow query.

Diagnose first, fix second:

1. Get EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN output for the query against the live DB (via a python script, not sqlite3 CLI since it is not on PATH). Look for SCAN TABLE route_aircraft or SCAN TABLE any large table without USING INDEX - that is the bottleneck.

2. Based on the plan, add a composite index. Most likely needed is on route_aircraft(origin_id, dest_id) or similar. Do NOT add a partial index (partial indexes do not support ON CONFLICT and have bitten us before). Add the index via a _migrate_* function in database/schema.py following the existing idempotent pattern, AND update SCHEMA_SQL.

3. Re-run EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN after the migration, confirm SEARCH USING INDEX, confirm the endpoint completes in <500ms warm.

4. Run ANALYZE after adding the index so the planner picks it up (ensure_analyze_stats or equivalent already exists - use it).

If EXPLAIN shows the query is already using an index and the 7s is from row volume or Python post-processing, STOP and report - the fix is different (likely a materialized cache or query rewrite) and should be its own task.
tasks.testStrategy: Before: curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/heatmap-panel with Measure-Command or time it in the profiling log. Record baseline. After migration: same endpoint must return in <500ms warm, <2s cold. Load the Global Heatmap page in a browser and confirm it now renders with data. pytest -q still passes. Run the migration on a fresh DB copy AND on the live DB copy and confirm idempotency (running it twice does not error).

tasks.title: Fix /api/extraction-deltas O(n²) Python diff
tasks.description: Endpoint returns in 19.8s with only 1.7ms spent in the DB and 5 queries. The remaining ~19s is Python work, almost certainly an O(n²) diff computation on the deltas list.
tasks.details:
Endpoint at dashboard/routes/api/extraction_deltas.py:49-50. Read the handler and identify the diff computation.

Typical O(n²) pattern is a nested loop where for each row in list A, the code searches list B for a match using `any()`, `next()`, or `[x for x in B if ...]`. The fix is to build a dict keyed by the matching field once (O(n)), then do O(1) lookups in the inner loop (O(n) total = O(n) overall instead of O(n²)).

Scope constraint: do NOT rewrite the SQL or restructure the endpoint shape. Just fix the Python diff. The output JSON must be byte-identical to the current output for the same input data - add a test that captures current output before the fix and asserts equality after.

If the computation is NOT a nested-loop diff (e.g., it is a JSON serialization cost on a huge payload, or a Python-side aggregation that should be pushed to SQL), STOP and report - that is a different fix with different tradeoffs.
tasks.testStrategy: Capture current JSON output for a representative request, save as a golden file under tests/. After the fix, assert the endpoint output matches the golden file byte-for-byte (or with a sorted-keys normalization if dict ordering matters). Measure endpoint latency before and after via the profiling log: target <500ms warm. pytest -q passes, ideally with a new golden-file test added (test count 175+).

tasks.title: Diagnose and fix /api/hub-summary cold ~6s
tasks.description: Endpoint at dashboard/routes/api/analytics.py:84-85 takes ~6 seconds on cold hits. Pre-existing, not yet investigated.
tasks.details:
Diagnose-first task. Do NOT apply a fix until the root cause is identified and reported.

1. Read the handler.
2. Add per-step timing inside the handler (or use the existing profiling middleware's query-level output) to identify whether the 6s is one slow query, many medium queries, Python post-processing, or template rendering.
3. For each slow SQL, run EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN.
4. Produce a diagnostic report with: handler code summary, timing breakdown (ms for each step), EXPLAIN output for every query, and a proposed fix category (index, query rewrite, materialized cache, Python refactor, or other).

Only AFTER the diagnostic is delivered should the fix be implemented, and only if it fits in the same task scope (single composite index or single query tweak). If the fix requires a materialized cache, a schema change, or touches more than ~3 files, STOP after the diagnostic and spin out a follow-up task.

Cold vs warm matters: measure both. 6s cold / <500ms warm is a cache-warming issue (different fix - touch SQLite cache or add a warmup on startup). 6s in both is a real query problem.
tasks.testStrategy: Diagnostic phase: deliver the report with measured timings and EXPLAIN output. Fix phase (if in-scope): cold hit <2s, warm hit <500ms. pytest -q passes 174+. Run the migration (if any) idempotently on fresh + live DB copies.

tasks.title: Fix ghost-hub architectural root in dashboard/db.py:235-242
tasks.description: Hub origin enumeration still groups route_aircraft by origin_id instead of reading from v_my_hubs. This is the architectural root of the original ghost-hub bug (which was previously patched at the symptom level only). Any hub deleted from my_hubs that still has route_aircraft rows will reappear as a ghost.
tasks.details:
HIGHEST-RISK task in this batch - do last. Touches the query that every hub-aware page downstream consumes.

1. Read dashboard/db.py:225-260 and identify the enclosing function. Understand what its callers expect: a list of hub IATAs? a list of (iata, id) tuples? with metadata like route counts? The v_my_hubs view and the route_aircraft grouping may currently expose different shapes.

2. Rewrite the function to read from v_my_hubs as its source of truth for WHICH hubs exist. If callers also need per-hub metrics (route count, total capacity, etc.), compute those by joining v_my_hubs to route_aircraft - NOT by enumerating from route_aircraft first.

3. Before committing, grep for every caller of this function across dashboard/ and confirm the new return shape is compatible. If any caller expects a shape only available via the route_aircraft grouping, either (a) preserve the metric by joining, or (b) flag that caller as needing its own follow-up and keep the new function's contract clean.

4. Add a test: insert a row into my_hubs, then delete it, and confirm it does NOT appear in the function's output even if route_aircraft rows for that origin_id still exist (simulating the ghost-hub scenario).

5. The is_active / last_extract_status filter from the original ghost-hub prevention spec: if v_my_hubs already filters on these, good; if not, check whether the prevention task-master spec was ever applied. Flag any gap but do not expand scope here.
tasks.testStrategy: New regression test: create a hub in my_hubs, populate route_aircraft rows for it, delete from my_hubs, call the audited function, assert the deleted hub is NOT returned. Run full pytest -q - must pass 174+ with the new test bringing it to 175+. Manually test Hub Explorer, /my-hubs, dashboard home - all hub lists must match what is in my_hubs (no ghosts). If the change breaks any caller that was depending on the old behavior, STOP and report BEFORE adjusting callers - that is a signal the scope is growing.
